title: 2x4 Outdoor Chaise Lounge
summary: A fixed-frame outdoor daybed lounge built around your cushion — 2x4 boxes for the base, arm, back, and foot, decked in cedar 1x6, with a universal cut list keyed to mattress size.
description:
This lounge is designed backwards from the cushion, and that's its best idea. The cut list is literally a formula — base rails at mattress length minus 3-1/2", cross members at mattress width minus 3" — with a worked example for a 30" x 75" cushion. Buy or measure the cushion FIRST, then cut. A lounge built to a plan dimension and married to a cushion that's two inches off looks wrong every single day; one built from the cushion fits like it was upholstered.

Unlike an adjustable chaise, this is a fixed daybed layout: a low 2x4 base frame, a taller box at the head end (the 'arm' you lean against), a matching back piece, and a low box at the foot. Each is a simple screwed 2x4 frame — build them as four separate boxes, then bolt the whole neighborhood together around the base. The decking is cedar 1x6 across the top; cedar fence pickets substitute if the budget says so, sanded well because bare legs land here.

Every joint gets glue plus 2-1/2" self-tapping exterior screws. The frames are simple enough that the glue matters — screwed-only outdoor furniture works itself loose racking through seasons of use and weather; glued joints don't.

Finish note straight from the source: use an exterior OPAQUE stain rather than paint. It looks painted, but it penetrates instead of filming, so it weathers by fading instead of peeling — on furniture that lives outside, that's the difference between refreshing a finish and stripping one.

tools.note: All straight crosscuts. A miter saw speeds up the repetition.
Non-negotiable if decking with fence pickets — bare skin lands on this.
The cushion is the spec sheet. Measure it before cutting anything.

materials.name: 2x4 lumber
materials.note: Buy long lengths — they cut with less waste against this list.

materials.name: Cedar 1x6, 6 ft
materials.note: Decking, ~71-1/2" each. Cedar fence pickets substitute if sanded well.

materials.name: Exterior opaque stain
materials.note: Opaque stain, not paint — it fades instead of peeling.

materials.name: Outdoor cushion or mattress, 30" x 75"
materials.note: Not in the cost estimate — prices swing wildly. Buy it FIRST; the frame is cut from its dimensions.

cutList.note: FORMULA: mattress length − 3-1/2". Shown for a 75" mattress.
Cut to match your actual base length.

steps.title: Measure the cushion, then cut
steps.body:
Lay the actual cushion down and take its real length and width — labels lie by an inch more often than they don't. Run the formulas (rails = length − 3-1/2", cross members = width − 3") and cut the base parts.

Then build the base: two long rails, four cross members spaced evenly, glue and 2-1/2" screws at every joint, two per joint. Square it by matching diagonals.

steps.title: Build the arm box
steps.body: Build the head-end arm assembly — the tall 26" legs with its cross members and the full-width cap — with glue and screws. This is the piece you lean against and set a drink on, and the joint where its cap meets the legs takes real leverage, so don't skimp the glue here.

steps.title: Build the back piece
steps.body: Repeat the same box construction for the back assembly — same joinery, per the diagram's proportions. Building these as separate complete boxes before any of them meet the base is what keeps every face flush at final assembly.

steps.title: Build the foot piece
steps.body: The low box for the foot end: 14" legs, same cross members, same glue-and-screws. Three boxes and a base, all waiting on each other.

steps.title: Assemble around the base
steps.body:
Stand the arm, back, and foot assemblies around the base and screw everything together with the 2-1/2" screws — through the base rails into the legs, and box-to-box where they meet.

Do this on flat ground and check that all four feet touch before the final screws. A lounge that rocks on a patio slab will find its wobble every afternoon at nap time.

steps.title: Deck, fill, and stain
steps.body:
Run the four cedar 1x6 deck boards along the top of the base with even gaps, fastened with the 1-1/2" screws — the gaps drain the inevitable rained-on cushion. Sand everything, hard, then fill screw holes with exterior filler and sand again.

Finish with an exterior opaque stain: painted look, penetrating behavior, fades instead of peels. Drop the cushion in — it fits exactly, because it designed the frame.

images.alt: Outdoor daybed-style chaise lounge with cushion, built from 2x4s with cedar decking
